coredata.dc:title: Poria cocos-derived carbon dots for cellular imaging, free radical scavenging and pH sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Yuan, Song
Luo, Yuanping
Jiang, Yuxiang
Xu, Wenjun
Cheng, Beijun
Peng, Zhili
coredata.dc:description: Green, economical preparation of carbon dots (CDs) from traditional Chinese herbal medicine Poria cocos via a one-step hydrothermal treatment was reported. The CDs exhibited excellent aqueous dispersibility, superior photoluminescence (PL) property and low cytotoxicity. Taking advantage of these properties, CDs were demonstrated as excellent multicolor bioimaging agents for the cellular imaging of MDA-MB-435S cells, with main distributions in the cell membranes and cytoplasm. Moreover, the CDs also exhibited good radical scavenging activity against 1,1-diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) thanks to their inheritance of characteristic functionalities from Poria cocos. Lastly, our study also shown that the PL of CDs was reversibly sensitive to pH in the range of 4.0 and 9.0, and a highly sensitive pH-sensing assay was developed exploiting this phenomenon. In summary, the study opened up new development paths for the modernization and vitalization of traditional Chinese medicine in biomedical research.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Poria cocos
Cellular imaging
Radical scavenging
pH sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of photoluminescence quantum yields for CDs

Chen acknowledges the financial support from the Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation under the grant number: ZR2020QB040 . Authors thank the Advanced Analysis and Measurement Center of Yunnan University for the sample testing service.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Illustration of the formation process of carbon dots from Poria cocos by hydrothermal treatment. (b) TEM images of CDs, inset is the HRTEM image showing the lattice fringe. (c) Particle size distribution histogram of CDs. (d) XRD pattern of CDs. (e) Raman spectrum of CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum (black line), fluorescence excitation (red line, λem = 450 nm), and emission spectra (blue line, λex = 360 nm) of the CDs dispersed in water at room temperature. The inserts show the photographs of the CDs dispersions under ambient light (left) and UV light of 400 nm (right), respectively. (b) Image of CIE coordinates showing the blue color of CDs. (c) Fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs obtained at different excitation wavelengths with a 20 nm increment from 300 to 520 nm. Inset shows the normalized emission spectra. (d) Decay curve of CDs (λex = 375 nm and λem = 450 nm).
(a) FTIR spectra of Poria cocos and CDs. (b) XPS survey spectrum of CDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of (c) C 1s, (d) O 1s, (e) N 1s.
The fluorescence images under (a and e) bright field, (b) 385 nm excitation, (c) 470 nm excitation and (d and f) 525 nm excitation of MDA-MB-435S cells incubated with CDs for 4 h.

(a) Scavenging of DPPH radicals as a function of time in the presence of 1000 μg/mL CDs. (b) Absorbance titration spectra of DPPH with gradual addition of CDs from 0 to 1000 μg/mL. (c) Scavenging ability of the CDs against DPPH radicals. (d) Established calibration curve showing the inhibition rates of CDs on DPPH at varied concentrations of 125–1000 μg/mL. (e) Proposed radical scavenging mechanism of CDs.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs at different pH (410 nm excitation). (b) Linearity of F/F0 versus different pH values, where F and F0 are the emission intensities of CDs at 410 nm for pH 1 and other pH, respectively. (c) Figure showing the reliability of the pH-sensing assay in which the actual pH values and calculated pH values from the calibration curve in Fig. 6b was compared. (d) Reversible pH response of the fluorescence behavior of CDs between pH 4 and 9.

head.ce:title: Poria cocos-derived carbon dots for cellular imaging, free radical scavenging and pH sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Yunnan Key Laboratory for Micro/Nano Materials & Technology, National Center for International Research on Photoelectric and Energy Materials, School of Materials and Energy, Yunnan University, Kunming 650091, China

National Center for International Research on Photoelectric and Energy Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Electron Microscopy Center, Yunnan University, Kunming 650091, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences), Jinan 250353, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences), Jinan 250353, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Green, economical preparation of carbon dots (CDs) from traditional Chinese herbal medicine Poria cocos via a one-step hydrothermal treatment was reported. The CDs exhibited excellent aqueous dispersibility, superior photoluminescence (PL) property and low cytotoxicity. Taking advantage of these properties, CDs were demonstrated as excellent multicolor bioimaging agents for the cellular imaging of MDA-MB-435S cells, with main distributions in the cell membranes and cytoplasm. Moreover, the CDs also exhibited good radical scavenging activity against 1,1-diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) thanks to their inheritance of characteristic functionalities from Poria cocos. Lastly, our study also shown that the PL of CDs was reversibly sensitive to pH in the range of 4.0 and 9.0, and a highly sensitive pH-sensing assay was developed exploiting this phenomenon. In summary, the study opened up new development paths for the modernization and vitalization of traditional Chinese medicine in biomedical research.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have garnered significant attention owing to their simple preparation, facile surface functionalization, remarkable optical properties and high biocompatibility [1,2], and have been widely applied for bioimaging, drug delivery and biosensing [3–5]. Currently, the main synthesis methods of CDs include hydrothermal/solvothermal, acid oxidation, laser ablation and electrochemical synthesis [6–9]. However, the traditional CDs synthesis processes generally involved with hazardous substances (i.e., strong acids, bases) or harsh reaction conditions (i.e., high temperature and pressure), which might cause serious environmental pollutions and also bring high risks to human health. As such, the green synthesis of CDs avoiding the use of toxic and harmful reagents is highly desired which could also endow as-prepared CDs with excellent biocompatibility and bioactivity [10,11]. In addition, biomass including milk, onion skin, lemon juice, black pepper and watermelon juice has become a promising precursor for green synthesis of CDs thanks to their renewable nature [12]. These biomass-derived CDs have exhibited excellent water dispersibility, biocompatibility and widely applied in biomedicine and drug delivery [13,14]. Interestingly, some studies have shown that enhancing the photoluminescence properties of CDs derived from green precursors could also improve their selectivity and sensitivity towards target molecules [15,16]. Therefore, it might be feasible to prepare CDs with good luminescent and physicochemical properties with green carbon precursors.

Meantime, Chinese herbal medicine has a long history and wide application in the field of traditional Chinese medicine. However, some drawbacks such as large particle size, slow dissolution rate, poor water solubility and stability may affect its therapeutic effect and bioavailability. To tackle these challenges, nanotechnology has been employed in traditional Chinese medicine research [17], leading to the emergence of CDs derived from traditional Chinese medicines. Compared to CDs synthesized from regular carbon precursors, Chinese herbal medicine-derived CDs often exhibited good biocompatibility and high efficacy [18–21]. As such, preparation of CDs from Chinese herbal medicines, especially from their residuals and wastes, is very promising for the modernization and vitalization of traditional Chinese medicine.

Herein, we reported a simple carbonization strategy for the preparation of nano-sized CDs from Poria cocos and their application as bioimaging, antioxidant and sensing agents. Poria cocos is a traditional Chinese herbal medicine extracted from the dried sclerotia of the fungus Poria cocos. It is known for its richness in bioactive compounds, containing two major classes of chemicals polysaccharides and triterpenoids [22,23] (Table S1). These different components contribute to the multifaceted pharmacological activities of Poria cocos, including antitumor, antioxidant, and antibacterial effects [24–26]. It is noteworthy that the as-prepared CDs not only inherited the antioxidant activity of Poria cocos, but also exhibited higher chemical and photostability as well as biocompatibility. In light of the excellent properties exhibited by the CDs, we conducted in vitro cell studies, demonstrating that the CDs were efficient biological imaging agents with no obvious cellular toxicity. Additionally, excellent pH sensor was developed from the CDs, with excellent linear relationship and cyclability in the pH range of 4.0–9.0, further expanding the biological applications of CDs.

Poria cocos were procured from the medicinal materials market (Wenshan, China). 1,1-diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) was obtained from Shanghai Titan Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China), while ascorbic acid (AA) was acquired from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Fetal bovine serum (FBS) was sourced from Capricorn Scientific Co., Ltd. (Hesse, Germany), and Roswell Park Memorial Institute medium 1640 (RPMI-1640), Minimum Essential Medium (MEM), and Trypsin were obtained from Life Technologies (Grand Island, NY, USA). Additionally, 3-(4,5-Dimethyl-2-thiazolyl)-2,5-diphenyl-2H-tetrazolium bromide (MTT) was procured from Sigma Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Unless otherwise stated, the reagents were used without further purification. Deionized (DI) water utilized in experiments was prepared using a Master Touch-S laboratory ultrapure water machine (Master Touch, Shanghai, China).

CDs derived from Poria cocos were synthesized using a simple hydrothermal method. Specifically, 1 g of Poria cocos powder was dissolved in 20 mL of DI water, the mixture was then evenly mixed, transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. The resulted suspension was allowed to cool naturally and subsequently centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 5 min, after which the large particles were removed via filtering with a 0.22 μm filter membrane. The brown filtrate was then freeze-dried 24 h to yield the final product as brown gelatinous samples.

The morphology of CDs was characterized using a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (Tokyo, Japan), the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were obtained using an accelerating voltage of 200 kV and a magnification of 800 K times. Fluorescence spectra were measured with a F97 Pro fluorescence spectrometer (Shanghai Prism Technology Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China). The frequency-domain lifetime was measured using an FLS10000 fluorescence lifetime spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK). The UV–vis absorption spectrum was recorded using a UV-2600 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan) in a 1 × 1 × 3 cm quartz cuvette at room temperature. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded using an FTIR spectrometer (Middle Age Walker, Thermo, KS, USA). The Raman spectra were measured using an inVia (Renishaw, UK) spectrometer. Additionally, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was obtained on an X-ray diffractometer (DX-2700BH, Haoyuan Instrument Co., Ltd., Dandong, China) at a wavelength (λ) of 0.15406 nm. To investigate the element composition of CDs, the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were obtained directly from the brown gelatinous samples using an X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (K-Alpha+, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA); the X-ray source was monochromatic Al Kα with a hν = 1486.6 eV photon energy operated at 6 mA × 12 kV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of photoluminescence quantum yields for CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The relative photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQYs) of CDs was determined using quinine sulfate (0.1 M H2SO4 solution) as the standard and calculated according to the following equation:
(1)
Q X
=
Q ST
k X
/
k ST
/
η x
2
/
η ST
2
where QX and QST are the PLQYs of the testing sample (CDs, unknown) and the standard sample (quinine sulfate, 0.56), respectively; k X and k ST are the slopes of the integrated fluorescence intensity vs. absorbance of the test samples (CDs) and the standard sample (quinine sulfate), respectively; η x and η ST are the refractive index of water and 0.1 M sulfuric acid solution, respectively, both of which were taken as 1.33.

The human breast cancer cell line MDA-MB-435S, human cervical cancer cell line Hela and smooth muscle cells were cultured in DMEM medium supplemented with 10 % FBS and 1 % penicillin-streptomycin at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere containing 5 % CO2 in a CO2 incubator.

The cell viability of Hela cells and smooth muscle cells following uptake of CDs was assessed using the MTT assay [27]. In brief, 90 μL each of Hela and smooth muscle cells were seeded in 96-well plates at a density of 7 × 103 cells per well and incubated at 37 °C in a 5 % CO2 humidified atmosphere for 24 h to ensure cell attachment. Subsequently, 10 μL of various concentrations of CDs were added to each well. After 48 h of incubation, 20 μL of MTT solution (5 μg/mL) was added to each well and the cells were further incubated for 4 h. The supernatants were removed from each well and 100 μL of DMSO was added to dissolve the formazan crystals. The plates were then shaken for 15 s before the absorbances at 570 and 630 nm were measured using a microplate reader (Multiska FC, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA) to calculate cell viability.

To prepare the samples, MDA-MB-435S cells were seeded at a density of 105 cells per well in 24-well plates containing 900 μL of DMEM and incubated for 24 h. Next, 100 μL of CDs solution (1 mg/mL) was added and further incubated for another 4 h. The cells were then washed three times with normal saline and fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde solution for 15 min, followed by another three washes with normal saline. Fluorescence images were captured using an inverted fluorescence microscope (NI-U, Nikon, Japan).

The DPPH free radical scavenging activity was measured based on a previously reported method [28] with slight modification. Firstly, ethanol dispersions of CDs with gradient concentrations were prepared, along with ethanol solutions of ascorbic acid and DPPH. Then, 1 mL of ethanol dispersions of CDs with different concentrations was added to 1 mL of ethanol solution of DPPH (0.1 mM). The mixtures were shaken well and kept in dark at room temperature for 30 min, after which the absorbances at 519 nm were measured and the radical scavenging activities of CDs towards DPPH were calculated using the following equation:
(2)
Inhibition
%
=
1 −
A sample
−
A control
/
A blank
× 100
where Asample represents the absorbance of DPPH in the presence of CDs (antioxidants), Acontrol represents the absorbance of CDs dispersion, and Ablank refers to the absorbance of DPPH solution only. Ascorbic acid was used as positive control, and the experiments were repeated three times in each group.

CDs dispersions of various pHs were prepared by mixing 500 μL of CDs dispersions with 2.5 mL of phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) solutions of different pH ranging from 1 to 12. The fluorescence emissions between 420 and 600 nm of these suspensions were then recorded by exciting with light of 410 nm and analyzed to establish the calibration curves. All measurements were performed in triplicate and averaged.

The CDs reported in this study were obtained as brown gels after a one-pot hydrothermal treatment with Poria cocos as carbon precursors (Fig. 1a). A series of characterizations were applied to study the morphological, structural, chemical and optical properties of CDs. Firstly, the morphology and size distribution of CDs particles were studied by TEM. Clearly, the CDs were well and homogeneously dispersed spherical nanoparticles with no obvious aggregations (Fig. 1b), which might be attributed to their strong negative surface charges as indicated by the Zeta potential (−24.7 mV, Fig. S1). According to the particle size distribution statistics, the diameters of these particles range from 1.5 to 4.5 nm, with an average diameter of 2.7 nm (Fig. 1c). High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) showed that these CDs particles had distinct lattice fringes with a lattice distance of 0.21 nm (Fig. 1b, inset), which was consistent with the (100)-lattice fringe of graphite.

To further elucidate their structural characteristics, we also characterized CDs with XRD and Raman spectra. As shown, the XRD pattern of CDs demonstrated a wide and broad diffraction peak at 21° (Fig. 1d), which was commonly observed for CDs and generally regarded as a sign of successful formation of CDs [29,30]. In the Raman spectrum, the D band representing the edge defect was found at 1350 cm−1 while the G band deriving from the sp2 carbon was located at 1580 cm−1 (Fig. 1e). Clearly, the extremely low ID/IG ratio (0.19) in CDs indicated that the existence of high degree of sp2 clusters and fewer structural defects. Collectively consider the analysis from TEM, XRD and Raman spectrum, it is reasonable to conclude that Poria cocos were successfully carbonized after hydrothermal treatment and CDs were formed.

Following the morphological and structural characteristics elucidations, we also studied the optical properties of CDs. As can be seen, the aqueous dispersion of CDs was brown under ambient light and turned bright blue upon excited by a 400 nm hand-held light (Fig. 2a, inset). In the UV–vis absorption spectrum, a characteristic absorption peak at 283 nm was observed, which could be attributed to the π-π* transition of CC [31] (Fig. 2a, black curve). The CDs dispersion could be excited by light with wavelengths ranging from 300 to 450 nm (Fig. 2a, red curve). With the optimum excitation at 360 nm, CDs exhibited strong blue fluorescence with the maximum peak centered around 450 nm (Fig. 2a, blue curve), the Commission Internationale de L'Eclairage (CIE) coordinates of which was calculated to be (0.18, 0.20), falling right into the region of standard blue light (Fig. 2b).

As expected, the sample demonstrated a typical excitation-wavelength-dependent emission (Fig. 2c), as the excitation wavelengths gradually shifted from 300 to 520 nm; the emissions of CDs demonstrated a clear redshift from 425 to 550 nm (Fig. 2c, inset). The tunable emissions of CDs might be attributed to the presence of CDs of various sizes in the sample or the existence of different emission sites on the CDs surface, both of which could increase the complexity of CDs excited states. The relative PLQYs of the CDs was determined to 1 %. Furthermore, the radiative decay curve of CDs was plotted (Fig. 2d) and the fluorescence lifetime was calculated to be 2.12 ns (Table S2). To our delight, the CDs fluorescence was highly stable as the emission positions and intensities remained basically unchanged after two-month-storage at ambient conditions (Fig. S2). Furthermore, the CDs fluorescence could also bear high ionic strength (Fig. S3), further highlighting the superior PL properties of CDs.

The chemical compositions and surface functionalities of CDs were further evaluated by FTIR and XPS (Fig. 3 ). As shown by the FTIR spectrum (Fig. 3a, black curve), various functional groups were observed in Poria cocos, such as OH/NH (3284 cm−1), CH (2901 cm−1), CO (1634 cm−1), CN (1366 cm−1) and CO (1035 cm−1). Interestingly, these peaks were also observed in CDs (Fig. 3a, red curve), suggesting that CDs might have inherited significant characteristics from the carbon precursor Poria cocos. The XPS spectrum of CDs demonstrated three main peaks at 534.1, 399.5 and 283.9 eV, corresponding to O 1s, N 1s and C 1s peaks, respectively (Fig. 3b). The atomic ratios of C, O and N atoms in CDs were determined to be 66.39, 32.25 and 1.26 %, respectively, which were in good agreement with the elemental compositions of Poria cocos (Table S3), indicating the hydrothermal process did not significantly alter the elemental compositions of the carbon precursor. The high-resolution spectrum of C1s could be fitted to three binding energies: namely CC/CC (284.7 eV), CO/CN (286.2 eV) and CO (287.9 eV) (Fig. 3c), while two peaks at 532.2 and 532.8 eV were observed in the high-resolution spectrum of O 1s, which are related to CO and CO, respectively (Fig. 3d). In the N 1s high-resolution spectrum, two nitrogen states were observed, namely the pyrrolic N at 399.6 eV and the graphitic N at 401.2 eV (Fig. 3e). Obviously, the results of XPS characterizations corresponded well with those of FTIR analysis, supporting the presence of abundant hydrophilic groups such as hydroxyl (OH) and carboxyl (COOH) groups on the surface of CDs, which should endow them with excellent aqueous dispersion property and thus favor their potential bioapplications.

With the systematic characterizations, we then tested the possibility to develop the CDs into excellent bioimaging agents. To begin with, the cytotoxicity of CDs on two different types of cells was carefully evaluated by MTT assay. To our delight, both Hela and smooth muscle cells maintained quantitative cell survival rates when incubated with CDs of different concentrations ranging from 31.25 to 250 μg/mL (Fig. S4). Furthermore, smooth muscle cells demonstrated 100 % cell viability even when the concentration of CDs was increased to 500 μg/mL (Fig. S4), highlighting the low cytotoxicity of the Poria cocos-derived CDs.

To probe the feasibility of CDs as a bioimaging agent, MDA-MB-435S cells were adopted as the cell model due to their high metastatic property and easy-to-culture characteristics. In the bright field, MDA-MB-435S cells cultured in the presence of CDs exhibited robust growth (Fig. 4a) and showed typical morphology (Fig. 4e), again highlighting the low cytotoxicity of CDs. Clearly, CDs could be quickly uptaken by MDA-MB-435S cells and the CDs-labeled cells exhibited bright blue (Fig. 4b), green (Fig. 4c) and orange (Fig. 4d) fluorescence under ultraviolet (385 nm), blue (470 nm) and green (525 nm) light excitation, respectively. Close observation revealed that CDs mainly distributed in the membrane and cytoplasmic region, as other regions showed little fluorescence (Fig. 4f). These results indicated that CDs had good cellular permeability, and could be effectively absorbed by MDA-MB-435S cells, accumulated in the cytoplasm through membrane-mediated endocytosis [32,33].

Thanks to the rich presence of polysaccharides, triterpenoids and flavonoids, Poria cocos are known for their antioxidant ability and could function as free radicals scavenger [34]. As characterized by FTIR and XPS above, CDs carried significant features from Poria cocos, thus we also carefully evaluated the free radical scavenging capability of CDs. DPPH is a stable nitrogen free radical with a characteristic absorbance peaked at 519 nm, and the intensity is linearly correlated to the interaction with antioxidants (i.e., CDs), thus DPPH has been widely applied for the quantitative evaluation of free radical scavenge ability of antioxidants [33,35]. To begin with, we first optimized the response time of the assay. As shown, the interaction of CDs with DPPH reached equilibrium after 30 min (Fig. 5a), as a result, 30 min was adopted as the standard response time for all the experiments following. To our delight, CDs exhibited excellent free radical scavenge capability towards DPPH, the absorbance of DPPH at 519 nm declined promptly as the concentrations of CDs increased, and a quantitative diminish was observed as the CDs concentration was promoted to 1000 μg/mL (Fig. 5b). Indeed, further addition of CDs did not cause significant changes indicating 1000 μg/mL was already saturated (Fig. 5c), and the final scavenging activity of CDs towards DPPH was calculated to be 95.4 %. Lastly, a linear calibration curve (y = 9.21 + 0.08x, R2 = 0.99) in the range from 125 to 1000 μg/mL was established by plotting CDs concentrations (x-axis) against the inhibition values (y-axis) (Fig. 5d), based on which the EC50 was determined to be 469 μg/mL.

According to the well documented studies [36–38], the antioxidant mechanism of CDs towards DPPH was cautiously proposed (Fig. 5e). As Characterization analysis revealed that CDs contained abundant carboxyl and hydroxyl surface-active groups. In the presence of these moieties, the unpaired electrons of DPPH radicals were firstly captured and transferred to CDs structure containing CC main chain, forming a new conjugated system. Subsequently, the structure of CDs was partially degraded, releasing acidic residues such as carbonates and bicarbonates, which could effectively consume the free electrons of the free radicals.

Maintaining an appropriate pH level is of utmost importance. On one hand, the stability and activity of many drugs and biomolecules are closely linked to the pH of their environment. On the other hand, pH has a significant impact on numerous biochemical reactions and metabolic processes in living organisms. Therefore, pH sensitivity and efficient detection are critical for proper monitoring and control of biological systems. In addition to their applications as bioimaging and radical scavenging agents, we were delighted to report that the CDs could also function as highly efficient pH sensor. Specifically, the PL emission intensities of CDs at 410 nm were correlated to pH, and gradually increased with the shift of pH from 1 to 9 (Fig. 6a). To better illustrate their mathematic relationship, we plotted a graph in which F/F0 was set at y-axis and pH value at the x-axis, where F0 and F denoted the emission intensities of CDs at pH = 1 and other pH, respectively. It could be clearly seen that the PL intensities of CDs and pH had a good linear relationship with pH between 1 and 9 and a standard calibration curve with a linear equation of y = 0.53 + 0.44x (R2 = 0.99) was established (Fig. 6b).

To demonstrate the reliability and accuracy of this sensing assay, five spike solutions of different pH values were prepared and their calculated pH values from the calibration curve were compared to their actual pH. To our delight, the assay seemed very reliable for all spike solutions. Specifically, the recoveries ranged from 97.89 to 105.14 %, with relative standard deviation (RSD) ranged from 1.02 to 5.30 % (Fig. 6c). Lastly, the assay was found to be highly adaptive as shown by the cyclability test in which the CDs emissions exhibited no deterioration after 6 cycles of acidification (pH = 4) and de-acidification (pH = 9) (Fig. 6d), highlighting the potential of the CDs-based sensing platform for highly sensitive and reliable monitoring of acidic and alkaline environments. To further demonstrate the superior performance of the assay, we have carefully compared the pH sensing performance of our Poria cocos-derived CDs to that of previously reported biomass-derived CDs (Table S4). As shown, the pH sensing assay reported in this manuscript demonstrated a wide linear range, excellent reliability and reversibility, which was superior to the assays based on other biomass-derived CDs reported in literature [39–43].

In this study, we reported the facile, green and economical synthesis of blue fluorescent CDs via the one-pot hydrothermal treatment of the traditional Chinese medicine Poria cocos. The morphology, structure, chemical and elemental properties of CDs were studied by a series of characterization methods. The CDs are spherical nanoparticles with a negatively charged surface, and there are abundant hydrophilic groups (i.e., carboxyl, hydroxyl) on the surface of CDs. CDs also possess high PL stability, good water dispersibility, low toxicity as well as good biocompatibility. As such, they were applied for the multicolor fluorescence imaging of cells utilizing their excitation-dependent fluorescence properties. Furthermore, CDs retained the antioxidant activity of the carbon precursor and exhibited good free radical scavenging activity against DPPH free radicals with a scavenging rate as high as 95.4 %. Lastly, the pH-sensitive CDs exhibited a linear and reversible fluorescence response to pH values between 4.0 and 9.0 with high recoveries and low RSDs, validating the reliability and accuracy of this pH-sensing assay. Overall, this study not only provided a new natural source for the synthesis of biomass-derived CDs, but also demonstrated the versatile applications of CDs for bioimaging, antioxidant and sensing, opening up new development paths for the modernization and vitalization of traditional Chinese medicine in biomedical research.

Song Yuan: Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Yuanping Luo: Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Yuxiang Jiang: Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Wenjun Xu: Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Beijun Cheng: Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Zhili Peng: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Z. Peng gratefully acknowledges the financial support from the Applied Basic Research Program of Yunnan Province under the grant number: 202301AT070147. B. Chen acknowledges the financial support from the Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation under the grant number: ZR2020QB040. Authors thank the Advanced Analysis and Measurement Center of Yunnan University for the sample testing service.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2023.110121.

Chem. Eng. J.
